figures.caption: Figure 5: Lighting-shift gradient on all four tasks (rows) for RoHIL vs. HIL-SERL, HG-DAgger, IBRL. Three metrics per row: success rate, intervention rate, mean episode length. Horizontal axis: shift intensity {0, 10, . . . , 100}%. RoHIL remains essentially flat across the gradient, while baselines degrade roughly monotonically.
Table 4: Cross-method comparison at 60% illumination shift (60 trajectories per cell). SR is success rate; “T” is mean successful-episode duration (s). Best per task in bold.

Figure 1: RoHIL overview. Starting from an online best policy that degrades under lighting shifts, RoHIL uses (a) world-model relighting to synthesise illumination-diverse transitions, (b) offline robust fine-tuning with LActor=LSAC+Lmse and LCritic=LBellman+Lfeat, and (c) IRR replay buffers for retention. The resulting policy preserves original-light performance while improving changed-light robustness across four real-robot tasks.

Figure 4: Sweep of the original-light fraction α in IRR on USB insertion. Rows show source/shifted lighting; columns show success, intervention rate, and episode length. The anchored objective (purple) dominates standard SAC (blue) and peaks at α=0.75.
Table 8: Policy training details for the circuit-breaker actuation task.
Table 9: Policy training details for the table wiping task.
Figure 2: The four manipulation tasks: (a) ram_insertion, (b) usb_insertion, (c) circuit_breaker, and (d) table_wiping. Per-task hardware setup is in Appendix B.
Figure 6: Hardware mounts and camera placements for the four tasks.
Table 7: Policy training details for the USB insertion task.
Table 6: Policy training details for the RAM insertion task.

Figure 3: USB insertion (α=0.75): training-iteration sweep over 30 000 offline steps. Standard SAC oscillates and ends well below the online best (0.57/0.73), while the anchored Bellman–actor objective rises steadily and peaks at 1.00/1.00 around 15 000 steps on both source and shifted lighting.

Figure 8: Visual comparison of Cosmos-Transfer1-DiffusionRenderer (top) and UniRelight (bottom) on a representative manipulation frame across four HDRI conditions. UniRelight exhibits stronger inter-frame temporal consistency; DiffusionRenderer is roughly 7× cheaper to run.
Figure 7: The ten illumination conditions used in our evaluation. Top: five HDRI environment maps. Middle: three task-light spotlight configurations. Bottom: two natural-window-light shifts.
